content.text:
{But the Lord said to Samuel, `Do not look on his appearance or on the height of his stature…\ For the Lord sees not as man sees: man looks on the outward appearance, but the Lord looks on the heart.'}{1 Samuel 16:7}
 {The determinant is a function that compresses a matrix into a single number, yet that number encodes remarkable geometric information.}{— Gilbert Strang}

The determinant is a function that assigns a single number to every 
square matrix. This number encodes crucial information: whether the 
matrix is invertible, how it transforms area or volume, and more.

content.title: Determinant of a $2 \times 2$ Matrix
content.content:
For a $2 \times 2$ matrix $A = \begin{bmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{bmatrix}$, 
the **determinant** is:
\[
\det(A) = |A| = ad - bc
\]

content.title: Computing $2 \times 2$ determinants
content.problem:
$$
\det\begin{bmatrix} 3 & 2 \\ 1 & 4 \end{bmatrix} &= (3)(4) - (2)(1) = 10 \\
\det\begin{bmatrix} 2 & 6 \\ 1 & 3 \end{bmatrix} &= (2)(3) - (6)(1) = 0
$$
The first matrix is invertible; the second is singular.

content.title: Minors and Cofactors
content.content:
For an $n \times n$ matrix $A$:

- The $(i,j)$-**minor**, denoted $M_{ij}$, is 
 the determinant of the $(n-1) \times (n-1)$ matrix obtained by 
 deleting row $i$ and column $j$.

- The $(i,j)$-**cofactor**, denoted $C_{ij}$, 
 is $C_{ij} = (-1)^{i+j} M_{ij}$.

content.text:
The signs of cofactors follow a checkerboard pattern:
\[

content.title: Determinant by Cofactor Expansion
content.content:
The determinant of an $n \times n$ matrix $A$ can be computed by 
expanding along any row or column:

**Along row $i$:**
\[
\det(A) = a_{i1}C_{i1} + a_{i2}C_{i2} + \cdots + a_{in}C_{in}
\]

**Along column $j$:**
\[
\det(A) = a_{1j}C_{1j} + a_{2j}C_{2j} + \cdots + a_{nj}C_{nj}
\]

content.title: Computing a $3 \times 3$ determinant
content.solution:
Expanding along row 1:
$$
\det(A) &= 1 \cdot C_{11} + 2 \cdot C_{12} + 3 \cdot C_{13} \\
&= 1 \cdot \det\begin{bmatrix} 5 & 6 \\ 8 & 9 \end{bmatrix}
- 2 \cdot \det\begin{bmatrix} 4 & 6 \\ 7 & 9 \end{bmatrix}
+ 3 \cdot \det\begin{bmatrix} 4 & 5 \\ 7 & 8 \end{bmatrix} \\
&= 1(45 - 48) - 2(36 - 42) + 3(32 - 35) \\
&= 1(-3) - 2(-6) + 3(-3) \\
&= -3 + 12 - 9 = 0
$$
The determinant is 0, so this matrix is singular.

content.text: Strategy: Choosing the Best Row or Column

content.title: Efficient Cofactor Expansion
content.content:
When computing a determinant by cofactor expansion:

1. Choose the row or column with the most zeros (each zero 
 eliminates a term).

2. For large matrices, first use row operations to create zeros, 
 then expand.

content.title: Strategic expansion
content.solution:
Column 3 has only one nonzero entry (the 2 in position $(3,3)$). 
Expanding along column 3:
\[
\det(A) = 0 \cdot C_{13} + 0 \cdot C_{23} + 2 \cdot C_{33} + 0 \cdot C_{43}
= 2 \cdot C_{33}
\]
Since $C_{33} = (+1) \det\begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 & 1 \\ 3 & 1 & 4 \\ 1 & 0 & 3 \end{bmatrix}$, 
we continue expanding along column 2 (which also has one nonzero entry).

content.title: Determinant of Triangular Matrices
content.content:
If $A$ is triangular (upper or lower), then $\det(A)$ equals the 
product of the diagonal entries:
\[
\det(A) = a_{11} \cdot a_{22} \cdots a_{nn}
\]
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
For an upper triangular matrix, expand along the first column. Only 
$a_{11}$ is nonzero, and its minor is the $(n-1) \times (n-1)$ upper 
triangular matrix in the lower right. By induction, the result follows.

marginNotes.text:
O Lord, you have searched me and 
known me! You know when I sit down and when I rise up; you discern 
my thoughts from afar.
